So, the purpose of the incubator, a large part of it, is to get you guys to get your own case studies or testimonials. It's pretty much one and the same, but if you get one of these and they're juicy, they can be worth millions of dollars if used properly inside of your business. So, we really want to make sure that when we are approaching clients and identifying the leads that we can onboard, that we see potential for case studies, that we're also protecting ourselves as an agency or a freelancer to make sure that when the time comes, we can actually collect or we're making that process of collecting those testimonials a lot easier for ourselves because a lot of the times what will happen is you will do the work, you'll get results, the client is happy. You ask them for a case study, sure, sure, I'll get it out to you next week and next week comes around in two weeks and three months and next thing you know, the campaigns aren't doing so well and now the client doesn't feel like recording something for you. We pretty much, most of us have been in a similar situation as that and it sucks because you can get really great results and yet not get that juicy piece of online asset for your business. So, here's some pointers to help you ask for case studies and leverage those properly inside of your business. It all starts with the pitch. So when you get clients into your pipeline, whatever prospecting method you are using at that time, what you want to do is identify the ones that you think are going to be able to blow up or you're pretty confident you can get them really great results. If you see a lead like that and you audited them properly, it's really important. You don't want to do free work for nothing, right? It's important that you audit them properly and you're confident you can get them results. Pitch them a performance-based model. What that is is essentially a revenue-based pricing model and what that means is it takes the risk off of the client so it makes it an easy sell. They're going to say yes to that, right? If it's structured properly. For you, it means that you get a chance to work with that client and an opportunity to build up your case studies. Now, if you've already got case studies and testimonials, please don't do this. This is just for those of you that are getting started out and don't have your own testimonial collateral or assets. Now, we also want to involve the case study or leverage the case study as part of our pitch when we're actually on a sales call. The way that you can pitch that is something like this. Well, the price for the services is $2,000. But I tell you what, if you are happy with the results after we deliver and get the outcome

## [03:01 - 06:05]

that you are seeking and based on what we're agreeing here, then I'm happy to take $500 off of this price today if you are willing to give me a testimonial at that stage. Now, you give them that discount and trust me, it's worth it. The case study or testimonial is going to be worth way more than that in your business. But now you can put that in the contract. In the terms of agreement, you can say the price is $1,500 and the customer agrees to a testimonial for that. If a testimonial is not delivered, the customer will owe $500 as per the agreement. So now you're almost protecting yourself in a way and weaving in those testimonials and case studies into your actual contract. If you haven't put that in a contract and you've got a client that you're working with with already, whom you're getting great results for and they're being unresponsive with your request for a case study, a little tip here that can often help is to also incentivize them through their invoice. So get in touch with them, have a conversation with them, explain the results that you're getting and say to them, hey, it would really help us if you could share the results that we've been able to achieve for you. I'm happy to take $1,500 off your next invoice for you to do so. Now here's some pointers on what to tell your clients to say because you want to make sure you get a good case study. The worst you can get is a client that says something, well, Kat is great to work with and she's such a nice girl and we have so much fun. They sound good, but they're not going to do anything to help your prospective leads annihilate their objections that they have and build the confidence and reassurance in what they're seeking when they're looking at case studies or testimonials. And that is, can you actually get results, right? People don't want best friends, they don't want to meet nice people, they want to get an outcome or a solution to their problem. And so you want your case studies to be as specific as possible. Now when we say specific, what we mean here is ideally a figure, a number. So that could be a financial return, it could be a revenue figure, it could be a timeframe even, it could be a ROAS, it could be percentage growth. But if you can get them to mention a number, then it will really, really light fuel under your case study. The three main questions that you typically want a client to address in a case study or a testimonial video, or a testimony video I should say if that's what you're asking for is where were you at before starting to work with us? Now the reason you want them to address this question is because it will address a lot
